prompt: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of 20 m/s. Taking g = 10 m/sВІ, what is the maximum height reached by the ball?
markScheme: B вҖ” Using vВІ = uВІ - 2gs: at maximum height v = 0, so 0 = 400 - 2(10)s вҶ’ s = 400/20 = 20 m.

prompt:
A 2 kg block slides along a frictionless horizontal surface at 5 m/s and collides with a stationary 3 kg block. After the collision the blocks stick together (perfectly inelastic collision).

(a) Calculate the velocity of the combined blocks after the collision. [2 marks]
(b) Calculate the kinetic energy lost in the collision. [3 marks]
markScheme:
(a) By conservation of momentum: mвӮҒuвӮҒ + mвӮӮuвӮӮ = (mвӮҒ + mвӮӮ)v
2 Г— 5 + 3 Г— 0 = (2 + 3)v
10 = 5v вҶ’ v = 2 m/s  [2 marks]
(b) KE before = ВҪ Г— 2 Г— 5ВІ = ВҪ Г— 2 Г— 25 = 25 J
KE after = ВҪ Г— 5 Г— 2ВІ = ВҪ Г— 5 Г— 4 = 10 J
KE lost = 25 - 10 = 15 J  [3 marks]
exemplarAnswer:
(a) v = (2Г—5)/(2+3) = 2 m/s
(b) KE_lost = 25 - 10 = 15 J. Energy is converted to heat/sound/deformation.

prompt: State Newton's three laws of motion and give one real-world example of each law.
markScheme:
First Law (1 mark + example): An object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted on by a net external force. Example: a book resting on a table stays still until pushed.
Second Law (1 mark + example): The net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration (F = ma). Example: a heavier car requires more force to accelerate at the same rate as a lighter car.
Third Law (1 mark + example): For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. Example: a rocket expels gas downward (action) and is propelled upward (reaction).
exemplarAnswer:
1st Law (Inertia): A stationary object stays at rest until a net force acts вҖ” e.g., a hockey puck slides indefinitely on frictionless ice.
2nd Law: F_net = ma вҖ” e.g., doubling the engine force on a car doubles its acceleration.
3rd Law: Forces come in equal and opposite pairs вҖ” e.g., swimming: you push water backward, water pushes you forward.

prompt: Which property of a wave determines its perceived pitch when heard as a sound?
markScheme: C вҖ” Frequency determines pitch. Higher frequency вҶ’ higher pitch. Amplitude determines loudness. Wavelength and wave speed are related to frequency by v = fО».

prompt: Explain the difference between constructive and destructive interference of waves. Under what conditions does each occur?
markScheme:
Constructive interference [2 marks]: occurs when two waves meet in phase (path difference = nО» where n is an integer); the amplitudes add together producing a larger resultant amplitude.
Destructive interference [2 marks]: occurs when two waves meet exactly out of phase (path difference = (n + ВҪ)О»); the amplitudes cancel (partially or fully), producing a smaller or zero resultant amplitude.
exemplarAnswer:
Constructive interference: waves arrive in phase (crest meets crest), path difference = nО»; resultant amplitude is the sum of individual amplitudes вҖ” e.g., bright fringes in Young's double-slit experiment.
Destructive interference: waves arrive in antiphase (crest meets trough), path difference = (n+ВҪ)О»; resultant amplitude is the difference вҖ” e.g., dark fringes in double-slit experiment or noise-cancelling headphones.

prompt: Three resistors of 6 О©, 3 О©, and 2 О© are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance of the combination?
markScheme: B вҖ” For parallel resistors: 1/R_total = 1/6 + 1/3 + 1/2 = 1/6 + 2/6 + 3/6 = 6/6 = 1. Therefore R_total = 1 О©.

prompt:
A sealed container holds 0.5 mol of an ideal gas at a temperature of 300 K and a pressure of 1.5 Г— 10вҒө Pa.

(a) Calculate the volume of the container. (R = 8.31 J molвҒ»В№ KвҒ»В№) [2 marks]
(b) The temperature is increased to 450 K at constant pressure. Calculate the new volume. [2 marks]
(c) State one assumption of the ideal gas model. [1 mark]
markScheme:
(a) pV = nRT вҶ’ V = nRT/p = (0.5 Г— 8.31 Г— 300) / (1.5 Г— 10вҒө) = 1246.5 / 150000 вүҲ 8.31 Г— 10вҒ»Ві mВі  [2 marks]
(b) At constant pressure, V/T = constant: VвӮӮ = VвӮҒ Г— TвӮӮ/TвӮҒ = 8.31 Г— 10вҒ»Ві Г— (450/300) = 8.31 Г— 10вҒ»Ві Г— 1.5 вүҲ 1.25 Г— 10вҒ»ВІ mВі  [2 marks]
(c) Any one of: gas molecules have negligible volume compared to the container; no intermolecular forces between molecules; collisions are perfectly elastic; molecules are in random motion.  [1 mark]
exemplarAnswer:
(a) V = nRT/p = (0.5 Г— 8.31 Г— 300) / 1.5Г—10вҒө вүҲ 8.31 Г— 10вҒ»Ві mВі
(b) VвӮӮ = 8.31Г—10вҒ»Ві Г— (450/300) вүҲ 1.25 Г— 10вҒ»ВІ mВі
(c) Molecules have negligible volume / no intermolecular forces.

prompt:
A 0.5 kg block of copper (specific heat capacity = 385 J kgвҒ»В№ KвҒ»В№) is heated from 20В°C to 80В°C.

(a) Calculate the thermal energy supplied to the copper. [2 marks]
(b) Explain, in terms of molecular motion, what happens to the copper when thermal energy is supplied. [2 marks]
markScheme:
(a) Q = mcО”T = 0.5 Г— 385 Г— (80 - 20) = 0.5 Г— 385 Г— 60 = 11,550 J вүҲ 11.6 kJ  [2 marks]
(b) The thermal energy increases the vibrational kinetic energy of copper atoms in the lattice; atoms vibrate with greater amplitude and frequency, increasing the average kinetic energy (and therefore temperature) of the material.  [2 marks]
exemplarAnswer:
(a) Q = 0.5 Г— 385 Г— 60 = 11,550 J
(b) Thermal energy increases the vibrational KE of lattice atoms; they oscillate with greater amplitude, raising the material's internal energy and temperature.
